skill.name: sast-horusec
skill.description: Guides multi-language static application security testing with Horusec, including local scans, CI/CD integration, secret detection, false positive handling, and report review.
skill.summary: Run Horusec SAST scans and review security findings across multi-language projects.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, environment, filesystem, and script patterns. Most findings are documentation examples or legitimate SAST workflow guidance, but the Docker socket mount, world-writable Docker socket advice, and pipe-to-shell installers are confirmed high-risk operational patterns. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, so the skill is not blocked but should not publish without revisions.

high_findings.title: Docker Socket Mounted Into Scanner Container
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill recommends running Horusec with /var/run/docker.sock mounted into the container. Docker socket access can give a container control over the host Docker daemon, which is a high-impact privilege boundary risk even when used for legitimate scanning.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact Docker socket mount appears in recommended local and CI examples. The pattern is operationally legitimate for some scanners, but the host-control impact is well understood and directly evidenced.

high_findings.title: World-Writable Docker Socket Permission Advice
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The troubleshooting section advises chmod 666 on /var/run/docker.sock. This grants local users broad access to the Docker daemon and can enable host compromise through container creation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and appears as a solution for permission errors. The surrounding note discourages sudo in CI, but it does not provide a safer Docker permission model.

high_findings.title: Pipe-To-Shell Installer Commands
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill and CI template include commands that download remote shell scripts and pipe them directly to bash. This creates supply-chain risk because the executed content is not pinned, verified, or reviewed before execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct curl-to-bash installer patterns. They target known project URLs, so malicious intent is not confirmed, but the execution pattern is still high risk.

medium_findings.title: Extensive Shell Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill is designed to run security scanners, Docker commands, package installers, and report-processing commands. This is expected for a Horusec workflow, but users could run commands against sensitive repositories or CI systems without sandboxing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are normal for SAST usage and do not show injection by themselves. The risk comes from execution context and the broad file access expected during source scanning.

medium_findings.title: CI Token And Repository Automation Exposure
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The CI template uses a GitHub token for Gitleaks and reads a generated report before posting a pull request comment. These are legitimate GitHub Actions patterns, but they require least-privilege permissions and careful artifact handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token is accessed through GitHub secrets and the file read targets a generated report, so this is not credential exfiltration. It remains relevant because CI automation handles repository metadata and security findings.

low_findings.title: Intentional Vulnerable Code Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded secrets, weak cryptography, XSS, C2, and reconnaissance terms appear inside security-rule templates or reference examples. They document what a scanner should detect rather than implementing malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding content labels the snippets as vulnerable examples, rule metadata, or framework mappings. No evidence found that these snippets execute as part of the skill.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs And Reference Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hardcoded URLs point to Horusec documentation, OWASP, CWE, and security tooling references. These are normal documentation links and do not by themselves indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are in reference lists and remediation text. They are not paired with secret collection or outbound data transfer in the inspected context.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Are Mostly Defensive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Environment variable references are used for GitHub Actions secrets or examples showing how to avoid hardcoded credentials. This is safer than embedding secrets directly, but users should still protect scan results.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use platform secret storage or compare an API key defensively. No evidence found that the skill sends environment values to an external endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Docker Socket Bind Mount
dangerous_patterns.description: The recommended Docker invocation binds /var/run/docker.sock into the scanner container, which can expose host-level Docker control.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bind mount is explicit in runnable examples and is a recognized host privilege risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: World-Writable Docker Socket
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting guidance uses chmod 666 on the Docker socket, granting broad Docker daemon access to local users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is exact and the security impact is direct. The surrounding text does not mitigate the permission change.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped To Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The Linux install and tfsec examples execute remote scripts directly through bash without checksum or signature verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present and executable. The domains appear legitimate, so this is unsafe guidance rather than proven malicious code.

content.value_statement: Security teams need consistent static analysis across mixed-language repositories. This skill guides Horusec scans, CI integration, secret detection, report review, and false positive handling.
actual_capabilities: Explains local Horusec scan workflows for multi-language repositories.
Shows CI/CD integration patterns for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins.
Covers git history secret scanning and credential rotation steps.
Describes severity thresholds, JSON output, and report parsing workflows.
Provides examples for false positive management with Horusec configuration.
limitations: It depends on Horusec, Docker, git, and related scanners being installed or available.
It does not verify whether every referenced scanner image or installer is trusted.
It can produce false positives that need human security review.
Some included Docker and installer commands require safer hardening before use.

use_cases.title: Pre-Commit Security Review
use_cases.description: Run Horusec locally before opening a pull request and review high-severity findings first.

use_cases.title: Pipeline Security Gate
use_cases.description: Add SAST, dependency, secret, container, and IaC scans to CI with retained security artifacts.

use_cases.title: Security Triage Workflow
use_cases.description: Review Horusec output, separate true positives from false positives, and document accepted risk.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Horusec workflow to scan this repository. Explain the command, expected output, and first triage steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review SAST Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Horusec report. Prioritize findings by severity, confidence, exploitability, and remediation effort.

prompt_templates.title: Add CI Security Scanning
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe CI plan for Horusec scanning. Include permissions, artifact handling, failure thresholds, and developer feedback.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Enterprise Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Horusec policy for a multi-repository organization. Cover exclusions, false positive governance, secret response, and audit evidence.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a local Horusec scan workflow before submitting a pull request.
output: A short scan plan with prerequisites and a recommended command.
A triage checklist that starts with critical and high findings.
A reminder to protect generated reports because they may contain secrets.

output_examples.input: A DevOps engineer wants Horusec in GitHub Actions.
output: A pipeline outline with minimal permissions and retained artifacts.
A failure policy based on severity threshold and team risk tolerance.
A note to avoid unsafe Docker socket and pipe-to-shell defaults where possible.

output_examples.input: A security engineer needs help managing repeated false positives.
output: A review process for validating each ignored finding.
A documentation model for risk acceptance decisions.
A recurring review cadence for ignored hashes and excluded paths.

best_practices: Run scans in isolated environments with least-privilege access to source code and secrets.
Treat Horusec reports as sensitive because findings can expose secret locations and vulnerable paths.
Document every false positive decision with owner, reason, expiration date, and review cadence.
anti_patterns: Do not mount the host Docker socket into scanner containers unless risk is formally accepted.
Do not pipe remote installer scripts directly to a shell in production workflows.
Do not suppress findings permanently without evidence, ownership, and periodic review.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It guides Horusec static analysis, CI integration, secret detection, report review, and false positive management.

faq.question: Does it run Horusec automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows and commands that a user or automation system can execute in the target environment.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects Horusec, Docker, git, and optional CI security tools depending on the workflow.

faq.question: Can it find real secrets?
faq.answer: Horusec can detect exposed secrets, including git history findings. Any exposed credential should be rotated immediately.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The documentation includes Docker socket mounting, chmod 666 Docker socket advice, and pipe-to-shell installers.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use isolated runners, avoid privileged Docker access, verify installers, and restrict access to reports and CI tokens.
